1:
"And it came to pass, when all the kings which were on this side Jordan... heard thereof;" The witnesses note the kings of Canaan hearing of Israel's conquests and stirring to oppose. The witnesses note that Gill notes all the kings remaining, save those of Jericho and Ai already slain, now governing the land of Canaan. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the kings hearing thereof. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the kings — of Canaan; the on this side Jordan — the land remaining; the heard thereof — of the conquests; the hardened, not softened — a savour of death unto death (2 Cor. 2:16).

The witnesses agree the verse opens the response of the Canaanite kings to the fall of Jericho and Ai — not repentance, but confederacy: "all the kings... heard thereof" and bestirred themselves to resist; the same tidings that drove the Gibeonites to seek peace hardened the kings to seek war, the one report working faith in some and defiance in others; it teaches that the same providence of God softens one heart and hardens another, the news of his mighty acts meeting men according to their disposition, "a savour of life unto life, and... of death unto death" (2 Cor. 2:16), the kings hearing yet hardening against the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the kings heard: The witnesses note the all the kings: of Canaan. The witnesses note the on this side Jordan: the land remaining. The witnesses note the heard thereof: of the conquests. The witnesses note the hardened, not softened: a savour of death unto death (2 Cor. 2:16). The expositors observe the all the kings: of Canaan. The expositors observe the on this side Jordan: the land remaining. The expositors observe the heard thereof: of the conquests. The expositors observe the hardened, not softened: a savour of death unto death (2 Cor. 2:16). The believer is taught to mark the all the kings, the on this side Jordan, the heard thereof, the hardened, not softened, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that the kings of Canaan heard of Israel's conquests — the same report hardening them to war that drove others to peace, God's acts meeting men according to their hearts, a savour of death to the defiant (2 Cor. 2:16).
2:
"That they gathered themselves together, to fight with Joshua and with Israel, with one accord." The witnesses note the Canaanite kings uniting with one accord to fight Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes they gathered to fight with Joshua. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set them gathering with one accord, while Gibeon took another course. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the gathered together — the kings united; the to fight with Israel — war on God's people; the with one accord — rivalries sunk; the a vain confederacy — no counsel against the LORD (Prov. 21:30).

The witnesses agree the kings, though many and divided, joined in common cause against God's people: "they gathered themselves together... with one accord," sinking their rivalries to make war on Israel; the union of the wicked against the church is a frequent thing, enemies otherwise at odds combining against the people of God; yet such confederacies, set against the LORD, are vain, for "there is no wisdom nor understanding nor counsel against the LORD" (Prov. 21:30); it teaches that the gathering of the nations against God's cause cannot prosper, the kings' one accord a confederacy against him who sits in the heavens, "the kings of the earth set themselves... against the LORD" (cf. Ps. 2:2).

So the witnesses make the verse with one accord: The witnesses note the gathered together: the kings united. The witnesses note the to fight with Israel: war on God's people. The witnesses note the with one accord: rivalries sunk. The witnesses note the a vain confederacy: no counsel against the LORD (Prov. 21:30). The expositors observe the gathered together: the kings united. The expositors observe the to fight with Israel: war on God's people. The expositors observe the with one accord: rivalries sunk. The expositors observe the a vain confederacy: no counsel against the LORD (Prov. 21:30). The believer is taught to mark the gathered together, the to fight with Israel, the with one accord, the a vain confederacy, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that the kings gathered with one accord to fight Israel — the union of the wicked against the church, yet a vain confederacy, for there is no counsel against the LORD (Prov. 21:30).
3:
"And when the inhabitants of Gibeon heard what Joshua had done unto Jericho and to Ai," The witnesses note the men of Gibeon, hearing of Jericho and Ai, taking a different course from the kings. The witnesses note that Gill notes Gibeon was a large and royal city, a metropolis with three others under it, perhaps governed by elders. The witnesses note that they heard what Joshua had done. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the inhabitants of Gibeon — a great city; the heard what Joshua had done — to Jericho and Ai; the a different course — peace, not war; the the dread of the LORD — the beginning of wisdom (Prov. 9:10).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites, hearing the same news as the kings, drew the opposite conclusion — that resistance was hopeless and life must be sought another way: "when the inhabitants of Gibeon heard," they resolved not to fight but to deceive; their fear of Israel's God, though it bred a crafty stratagem, was a kind of faith in his power, like Rahab's, that the LORD would give the land; it teaches that some, hearing of God's judgments, are wise enough to seek peace though by wrong means, the dread of the LORD driving them to spare their lives, "the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom" (Prov. 9:10), Gibeon choosing submission over war.

So the witnesses make the verse Gibeon heard: The witnesses note the the inhabitants of Gibeon: a great city. The witnesses note the heard what Joshua had done: to Jericho and Ai. The witnesses note the a different course: peace, not war. The witnesses note the the dread of the LORD: the beginning of wisdom (Prov. 9:10). The expositors observe the the inhabitants of Gibeon: a great city. The expositors observe the heard what Joshua had done: to Jericho and Ai. The expositors observe the a different course: peace, not war. The expositors observe the the dread of the LORD: the beginning of wisdom (Prov. 9:10). The believer is taught to mark the the inhabitants of Gibeon, the heard what Joshua had done, the a different course, the the dread of the LORD, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that Gibeon, hearing of Jericho and Ai, chose to seek peace by craft — their fear of Israel's God a kind of faith, the dread of the LORD driving them to spare their lives (Prov. 9:10).
4:
"They did work wilily, and went and made as if they had been ambassadors, and took old sacks upon their asses, and wine bottles, old, and rent, and bound up;" The witnesses note the Gibeonites working craftily, feigning to be ambassadors from a far country with old, worn provisions. The witnesses note that Gill notes they acted craftily, with much cunning, in a different way from the other nations. The witnesses note that they took old sacks and old, rent wine bottles. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the did work wilily — the careful fraud; the feigned ambassadors — from a far country; the old sacks — the props of the lie; the old, rent bottles — craft in every detail (2 Cor. 2:11).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites laid a careful and clever fraud to feign a long journey: "they did work wilily... took old sacks... wine bottles, old, and rent," dressing the lie in every detail to seem ambassadors from far; the elaborate stratagem shows the lengths to which fear drove them, and the craft of which men are capable; though their motive — to save their lives — was understandable, the means was deceit; it teaches that even a right end does not justify a false means, and warns the people of God to beware the wiles of the crafty, "we are not ignorant of his devices" (2 Cor. 2:11), the Gibeonites' guile a snare laid with care.

So the witnesses make the verse they worked wilily: The witnesses note the did work wilily: the careful fraud. The witnesses note the feigned ambassadors: from a far country. The witnesses note the old sacks: the props of the lie. The witnesses note the old, rent bottles: craft in every detail (2 Cor. 2:11). The expositors observe the did work wilily: the careful fraud. The expositors observe the feigned ambassadors: from a far country. The expositors observe the old sacks: the props of the lie. The expositors observe the old, rent bottles: craft in every detail (2 Cor. 2:11). The believer is taught to mark the did work wilily, the feigned ambassadors, the old sacks, the old, rent bottles, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites worked wilily, feigning ambassadors with worn provisions — fear driving them to elaborate deceit, a right end not justifying a false means, the guile a snare laid with care (2 Cor. 2:11).
5:
"And old shoes and clouted upon their feet, and old garments upon them; and all the bread of their provision was dry and mouldy." The witnesses note the worn shoes, old garments, and dry, mouldy bread completing the false show of a long journey. The witnesses note that Gill notes the old shoes, worn out and patched with various pieces of leather. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the sandals pretended to be worn out by long and difficult travelling. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the old clouted shoes — patched for the lie; the old garments — the worn disguise; the dry and mouldy bread — feigning a long journey; the appearances misleading — the prudent looks well to his going (Prov. 14:15).

The witnesses agree the disguise was complete to the last detail — patched shoes, old garments, and "bread... dry and mouldy" — all to support the tale of a far journey: every prop was chosen to make the lie convincing; the thoroughness of the deception shows how persuasively falsehood can clothe itself, and how easily appearances may mislead; it teaches the people of God to look beyond the outward show and not to judge by appearance alone, for "the simple believeth every word: but the prudent man looketh well to his going" (Prov. 14:15), the mouldy bread and worn shoes a studied counterfeit.

So the witnesses make the verse the false show: The witnesses note the old clouted shoes: patched for the lie. The witnesses note the old garments: the worn disguise. The witnesses note the dry and mouldy bread: feigning a long journey. The witnesses note the appearances misleading: the prudent looks well to his going (Prov. 14:15). The expositors observe the old clouted shoes: patched for the lie. The expositors observe the old garments: the worn disguise. The expositors observe the dry and mouldy bread: feigning a long journey. The expositors observe the appearances misleading: the prudent looks well to his going (Prov. 14:15). The believer is taught to mark the old clouted shoes, the old garments, the dry and mouldy bread, the appearances misleading, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that worn shoes, old garments, and mouldy bread completed the false show — falsehood persuasively clothed, the people of God to look beyond appearance, the prudent looking well to his going (Prov. 14:15).
6:
"And they went to Joshua unto the camp at Gilgal, and said unto him, and to the men of Israel, We be come from a far country: now therefore make ye a league with us." The witnesses note the Gibeonites coming to Gilgal with their false claim and asking for a league. The witnesses note that Gill notes the army had returned to Gilgal after Jericho and Ai, and there the Gibeonites applied to them. The witnesses note that Clarke sets the request, make ye a league with us. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the came to Gilgal — to the camp; the from a far country — the central lie; the make ye a league — the suit pressed; the framed to the rule — leagues with the far allowed (Deut. 20:15).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites came to the camp and pressed their suit with the central falsehood: "we be come from a far country: now... make ye a league with us"; the lie of the "far country" was the very thing on which all turned, for a league with the near Canaanites was forbidden, but with distant nations allowed (Deut. 20:15); by this single deceit they sought to slip within the law's exception; it teaches the craft that frames its falsehood to fit the rule, and warns that a plausible plea may conceal a forbidden thing, the Gibeonites pressing for a covenant on a counterfeit ground.

So the witnesses make the verse make a league: The witnesses note the came to Gilgal: to the camp. The witnesses note the from a far country: the central lie. The witnesses note the make ye a league: the suit pressed. The witnesses note the framed to the rule: leagues with the far allowed (Deut. 20:15). The expositors observe the came to Gilgal: to the camp. The expositors observe the from a far country: the central lie. The expositors observe the make ye a league: the suit pressed. The expositors observe the framed to the rule: leagues with the far allowed (Deut. 20:15). The believer is taught to mark the came to Gilgal, the from a far country, the make ye a league, the framed to the rule, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites came to Gilgal claiming a far country and asking a league — the lie framed to fit the law's exception, a plausible plea concealing a forbidden thing (Deut. 20:15).
7:
"And the men of Israel said unto the Hivites, Peradventure ye dwell among us; and how shall we make a league with you?" The witnesses note Israel's suspicion — perhaps these are near neighbours with whom no league may be made. The witnesses note that Gill notes they did not yet know them to be Hivites, but so they afterwards appeared; the name signifies "serpents." The witnesses note that they asked how a league could be made if they dwelt among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the men of Israel said — rightly wary; the peradventure ye dwell among us — suspecting the truth; the how make a league — the unlawful covenant feared; the doubt not pursued — prove all things (1 Thess. 5:21).

The witnesses agree the men of Israel were rightly wary, half-suspecting the truth: "peradventure ye dwell among us; and how shall we make a league with you?"; their caution was sound, for they knew a covenant with the near Canaanites was unlawful, and they pressed the very point that should have unmasked the fraud; yet they did not push the inquiry to the end, nor take the one sure step of asking God; it teaches that a measure of godly suspicion is wise in dealing with the plausible, but that suspicion must be carried through to certainty, "prove all things" (1 Thess. 5:21), Israel's right doubt left half-pursued.

So the witnesses make the verse peradventure ye dwell among us: The witnesses note the the men of Israel said: rightly wary. The witnesses note the peradventure ye dwell among us: suspecting the truth. The witnesses note the how make a league: the unlawful covenant feared. The witnesses note the doubt not pursued: prove all things (1 Thess. 5:21). The expositors observe the the men of Israel said: rightly wary. The expositors observe the peradventure ye dwell among us: suspecting the truth. The expositors observe the how make a league: the unlawful covenant feared. The expositors observe the doubt not pursued: prove all things (1 Thess. 5:21). The believer is taught to mark the the men of Israel said, the peradventure ye dwell among us, the how make a league, the doubt not pursued, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that Israel suspected the Gibeonites might be near neighbours — a godly wariness, yet left half-pursued, suspicion not carried through to certainty, failing to prove all things (1 Thess. 5:21).
8:
"And they said unto Joshua, We are thy servants. And Joshua said unto them, Who are ye? and from whence come ye?" The witnesses note the Gibeonites professing submission, and Joshua pressing them whence they came. The witnesses note that Gill notes "we are thy servants" was said in great humility, willing to do anything he should enjoin, though not meaning to be subjects. The witnesses note that Joshua asked who and whence they were. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we are thy servants — feigned submission; the Joshua asked — who are ye; the from whence come ye — pressing for truth; the wisdom from God — let him ask of God (James 1:5).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites met suspicion with a show of submission, and Joshua probed further: "we are thy servants," they said in feigned humility, and Joshua, still unsatisfied, asked "who are ye? and from whence come ye?"; the leader's questioning was right and shrewd, pressing for the truth; yet the answer he sought was one God alone could surely give, and the inquiry, however prudent, stopped short of the LORD; it teaches that human probing, even at its wisest, cannot always pierce a practised lie, and that the surest light is to be sought from God, "if any of you lack wisdom, let him ask of God" (James 1:5), Joshua's question right but his counsel unsought from heaven.

So the witnesses make the verse who are ye: The witnesses note the we are thy servants: feigned submission. The witnesses note the Joshua asked: who are ye. The witnesses note the from whence come ye: pressing for truth. The witnesses note the wisdom from God: let him ask of God (James 1:5). The expositors observe the we are thy servants: feigned submission. The expositors observe the Joshua asked: who are ye. The expositors observe the from whence come ye: pressing for truth. The expositors observe the wisdom from God: let him ask of God (James 1:5). The believer is taught to mark the we are thy servants, the Joshua asked, the from whence come ye, the wisdom from God, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites professed submission and Joshua pressed them whence they came — his probing right but unable to pierce the lie, the surest light to be sought from God who gives wisdom (James 1:5).
9:
"And they said unto him, From a very far country thy servants are come because of the name of the LORD thy God: for we have heard the fame of him, and all that he did in Egypt," The witnesses note the Gibeonites grounding their suit on the fame of the LORD, all he did in Egypt. The witnesses note that Gill notes they magnified the "far country" in stronger terms, careful not to name any country lest questions betray them. The witnesses note that they came because of the name of the LORD and his fame. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from a far country — the magnified lie; the because of the name — a true confession; the the fame of him — his acts in Egypt; the truth in a false cause — many come in my name (Matt. 24:5).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites cloaked their lie in true and reverent words about the LORD: "from a very far country... because of the name of the LORD thy God: for we have heard the fame of him"; their report of God's acts in Egypt was true, and their professed motive — the fame of the LORD — touched the very thing that should have moved Israel; the mingling of true confession with false pretence made the deceit the more persuasive; it teaches that error often borrows the language of truth, and that fair words of God may yet serve a false design, the Gibeonites owning the LORD's fame even as they lied, "many shall come in my name" (cf. Matt. 24:5) — true words in a false cause.

So the witnesses make the verse the fame of the LORD: The witnesses note the from a far country: the magnified lie. The witnesses note the because of the name: a true confession. The witnesses note the the fame of him: his acts in Egypt. The witnesses note the truth in a false cause: many come in my name (Matt. 24:5). The expositors observe the from a far country: the magnified lie. The expositors observe the because of the name: a true confession. The expositors observe the the fame of him: his acts in Egypt. The expositors observe the truth in a false cause: many come in my name (Matt. 24:5). The believer is taught to mark the from a far country, the because of the name, the the fame of him, the truth in a false cause, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites grounded their suit on the fame of the LORD and his acts in Egypt — true confession mingled with false pretence, error borrowing the language of truth, fair words in a false cause (Matt. 24:5).
10:
"And all that he did to the two kings of the Amorites that were beyond Jordan, to Sihon king of Heshbon, and to Og king of Bashan..." The witnesses note the Gibeonites citing the defeat of Sihon and Og — but not Jericho and Ai, to keep up the deception. The witnesses note that Gill notes the two kings of the Amorites beyond Jordan, on the other side from Gilgal. The witnesses note that they spoke of Sihon and Og. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Sihon and Og — the distant victories; the beyond Jordan — the older conquests; the Jericho omitted — to hide their nearness; the craft choosing its evidence — a false witness speaking lies (Prov. 6:19).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites cunningly named only the older and more distant victories — Sihon and Og beyond Jordan — and pointedly omitted the recent fall of Jericho and Ai: to have mentioned these latest conquests, news of which could not have reached a "far country" so soon, would have betrayed their nearness; the careful selection of what to tell shows the calculated craft of the lie, true facts marshalled to support a falsehood; it teaches how artfully deceit chooses its evidence, telling enough truth to persuade and hiding what would expose it, "a false witness that speaketh lies" (Prov. 6:19) clothing his deceit in selected truths, the Gibeonites silent on Jericho by design.

So the witnesses make the verse Sihon and Og: The witnesses note the Sihon and Og: the distant victories. The witnesses note the beyond Jordan: the older conquests. The witnesses note the Jericho omitted: to hide their nearness. The witnesses note the craft choosing its evidence: a false witness speaking lies (Prov. 6:19). The expositors observe the Sihon and Og: the distant victories. The expositors observe the beyond Jordan: the older conquests. The expositors observe the Jericho omitted: to hide their nearness. The expositors observe the craft choosing its evidence: a false witness speaking lies (Prov. 6:19). The believer is taught to mark the Sihon and Og, the beyond Jordan, the Jericho omitted, the craft choosing its evidence, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites cited Sihon and Og but omitted Jericho and Ai — the calculated craft of the lie, telling enough truth to persuade and hiding what would expose it, a false witness clothing deceit in selected truths (Prov. 6:19).
11:
"Wherefore our elders and all the inhabitants of our country spake to us, saying, Take victuals... and go to meet them, and say unto them, We are your servants..." The witnesses note the Gibeonites' tale of being sent by their elders — all of it false, contrived to deceive. The witnesses note that Gill notes the elders and all the inhabitants of their country. The witnesses note that Clarke notes all this, to the end of v13, was false, contrived merely to deceive Israel and save their lives. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the our elders spake — the invented commission; the take victuals — the detailed tale; the we are your servants — the false errand; the all of it false — the deceitful heart (Jer. 17:9).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites built up a circumstantial tale of being commissioned by their elders, which Clarke bluntly marks "was false, contrived merely... to deceive": the full and detailed account of their sending lent the lie an air of truth, falsehood elaborated into a story; the readiness with which they piled detail on detail shows how deceit fortifies itself with invented particulars; it teaches that a lie, to be believed, dresses itself in circumstance, and warns against being taken in by a fluent and detailed tale, for "the heart is deceitful above all things" (Jer. 17:9), the Gibeonites' whole errand a fabrication to save their lives.

So the witnesses make the verse our elders sent us: The witnesses note the our elders spake: the invented commission. The witnesses note the take victuals: the detailed tale. The witnesses note the we are your servants: the false errand. The witnesses note the all of it false: the deceitful heart (Jer. 17:9). The expositors observe the our elders spake: the invented commission. The expositors observe the take victuals: the detailed tale. The expositors observe the we are your servants: the false errand. The expositors observe the all of it false: the deceitful heart (Jer. 17:9). The believer is taught to mark the our elders spake, the take victuals, the we are your servants, the all of it false, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites told a circumstantial tale of being sent by their elders — all of it false, deceit fortified with invented particulars, the heart deceitful above all things (Jer. 17:9).
12:
"This our bread we took hot for our provision out of our houses on the day we came forth to go unto you; but now, behold, it is dry, and it is mouldy:" The witnesses note the Gibeonites pointing to their mouldy bread as proof of a long journey. The witnesses note that Gill notes these are the words of the ambassadors to Israel, pointing to the bread they pretended was newly baked when they set out. The witnesses note that it was now dry and mouldy. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the our bread we took hot — the feigned freshness; the out of our houses — at the journey's start; the now dry and mouldy — the visible proof; the the eye deceived — walk by faith, not sight (2 Cor. 5:7).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites produced their mouldy bread as visible evidence of the long journey they feigned: "this our bread we took hot... but now... it is dry, and... mouldy"; the appeal to tangible proof, the very loaves before Israel's eyes, was the most persuasive stroke of the deceit, falsehood seeming confirmed by sight; it teaches that the senses may be deceived as readily as the ear, and that visible evidence, when its source is unknown, may mislead as surely as a spoken lie; the people of God must not lean on appearances, "walk by faith, not by sight" (2 Cor. 5:7), the mouldy bread a false witness to the eye.

So the witnesses make the verse the mouldy bread: The witnesses note the our bread we took hot: the feigned freshness. The witnesses note the out of our houses: at the journey's start. The witnesses note the now dry and mouldy: the visible proof. The witnesses note the the eye deceived: walk by faith, not sight (2 Cor. 5:7). The expositors observe the our bread we took hot: the feigned freshness. The expositors observe the out of our houses: at the journey's start. The expositors observe the now dry and mouldy: the visible proof. The expositors observe the the eye deceived: walk by faith, not sight (2 Cor. 5:7). The believer is taught to mark the our bread we took hot, the out of our houses, the now dry and mouldy, the the eye deceived, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites pointed to their mouldy bread as proof of a long journey — visible evidence deceiving the eye as a lie the ear, the people of God to walk by faith and not by sight (2 Cor. 5:7).
13:
"And these bottles of wine, which we filled, were new; and, behold, they be rent: and these our garments and our shoes are become old by reason of the very long journey." The witnesses note the rent wine-bottles and worn garments offered as further proof of the long journey. The witnesses note that Gill notes the bottles, filled new on the day they came out, now rent. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the bottles were leathern. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the bottles were new — now rent; the garments and shoes — become old; the the very long journey — the heaped proofs; the false evidence persuading — the prudent crowned with knowledge (Prov. 14:18).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites pressed every prop of their disguise — rent leather bottles, worn garments and shoes — as conclusive proof of "the very long journey": the heaping of evidence, each item seeming to confirm the rest, completed the persuasion; the cumulative weight of false proofs overcame the wariness Israel had shown; it teaches how a deceit, fortified at every point, can carry conviction against good sense, and how needful it is to bring even the most convincing case before God; the people were swayed by the mass of seeming proof, when one inquiry of the LORD would have undone it all, "the prudent are crowned with knowledge" (Prov. 14:18) — the worn garments a studied lie.

So the witnesses make the verse the worn garments: The witnesses note the bottles were new: now rent. The witnesses note the garments and shoes: become old. The witnesses note the the very long journey: the heaped proofs. The witnesses note the false evidence persuading: the prudent crowned with knowledge (Prov. 14:18). The expositors observe the bottles were new: now rent. The expositors observe the garments and shoes: become old. The expositors observe the the very long journey: the heaped proofs. The expositors observe the false evidence persuading: the prudent crowned with knowledge (Prov. 14:18). The believer is taught to mark the bottles were new, the garments and shoes, the the very long journey, the false evidence persuading, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that the rent bottles and worn garments were offered as proof of the long journey — the cumulative weight of false proofs overcoming Israel's wariness, one inquiry of the LORD would have undone it all (Prov. 14:18).
14:
"And the men took of their victuals, and asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD." The witnesses note the fatal failure — Israel examined the provisions but did not ask counsel of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the princes took of their victuals, not to eat, but to examine whether the bread was as mouldy as pretended. The witnesses note that they asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the took of their victuals — examined the bread; the trusting their own eyes — not God; the asked not counsel — the LORD unconsulted; the the root of the error — acknowledge him and he directs (Prov. 3:6).

The witnesses agree this verse names the very root of Israel's error — they trusted their own examination and "asked not counsel at the mouth of the LORD": they looked at the mouldy bread but did not look to God, who alone could have unmasked the fraud through the high priest and the Urim; the neglect of prayer, when the means of seeking God was at hand, was the failure that led to the rash league; it teaches that the surest safeguard against deception is to bring every doubtful matter before God, and that leaning on our own judgment, however careful, will betray us, "in all thy ways acknowledge him, and he shall direct thy paths" (Prov. 3:6) — the LORD's counsel unsought, and Israel ensnared.

So the witnesses make the verse asked not counsel: The witnesses note the took of their victuals: examined the bread. The witnesses note the trusting their own eyes: not God. The witnesses note the asked not counsel: the LORD unconsulted. The witnesses note the the root of the error: acknowledge him and he directs (Prov. 3:6). The expositors observe the took of their victuals: examined the bread. The expositors observe the trusting their own eyes: not God. The expositors observe the asked not counsel: the LORD unconsulted. The expositors observe the the root of the error: acknowledge him and he directs (Prov. 3:6). The believer is taught to mark the took of their victuals, the trusting their own eyes, the asked not counsel, the the root of the error, and to ask counsel at the mouth of the LORD. The verse thus records that Israel examined the provisions but asked not counsel of the LORD — the very root of the error, leaning on their own judgment instead of seeking God, who would have unmasked the fraud, acknowledge him and he shall direct thy paths (Prov. 3:6).
15:
"And Joshua made peace with them, and made a league with them, to let them live: and the princes of the congregation sware unto them." The witnesses note Joshua making peace and a league with the Gibeonites, the princes confirming it by oath. The witnesses note that Gill notes the peace was made on the princes' report that they had examined the provisions and found them as pretended. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the league made and the princes swearing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the made peace — with the Gibeonites; the a league to let them live — the covenant struck; the the princes sware — sealed by oath; the a binding vow — sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not (Ps. 15:4).

The witnesses agree the deceit succeeded — a league was struck and bound with an oath: "Joshua made peace... and the princes... sware unto them"; the covenant, made without seeking God and on the strength of a fraud, was yet sealed with a solemn oath in the name of the LORD; once sworn, that oath would bind, however it was obtained; it teaches both the danger of acting without God's counsel and the sacredness of a vow once made, the princes' oath now a bond they could not lightly break, for he that "sweareth to his own hurt... changeth not" (Ps. 15:4), the rash league sealed beyond recall.

So the witnesses make the verse the league sworn: The witnesses note the made peace: with the Gibeonites. The witnesses note the a league to let them live: the covenant struck. The witnesses note the the princes sware: sealed by oath. The witnesses note the a binding vow: sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not (Ps. 15:4). The expositors observe the made peace: with the Gibeonites. The expositors observe the a league to let them live: the covenant struck. The expositors observe the the princes sware: sealed by oath. The expositors observe the a binding vow: sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not (Ps. 15:4). The believer is taught to mark the made peace, the a league to let them live, the the princes sware, the a binding vow, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that Joshua made a league and the princes sware to the Gibeonites — a covenant struck without God's counsel yet bound by a sacred oath, sworn even to their own hurt and not to be changed (Ps. 15:4).
16:
"And it came to pass at the end of three days after they had made a league with them, that they heard that they were their neighbours, and that they dwelt among them." The witnesses note the discovery, three days later, that the Gibeonites were near neighbours after all. The witnesses note that Gill notes the league seems to have been made the same day; the Gibeonites were in haste lest they be discovered, and Joshua and the princes took no pains to inquire. The witnesses note that they heard the Gibeonites dwelt among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the at the end of three days — the swift discovery; the they heard — the truth came out; the their neighbours — near, not far; the too late — your sin will find you out (Num. 32:23).

The witnesses agree the truth came out quickly, but too late — "at the end of three days... they heard that they were their neighbours"; the very thing that one inquiry of God would have revealed before the league was now discovered after it, when the oath was already sworn; the swift unmasking shows how needless the deception had been, had they but asked counsel; it teaches that sin done in haste is repented at leisure, and that the truth withheld for a moment is not hidden long, "be sure your sin will find you out" (Num. 32:23), the fraud discovered when it could no longer be undone.

So the witnesses make the verse they were neighbours: The witnesses note the at the end of three days: the swift discovery. The witnesses note the they heard: the truth came out. The witnesses note the their neighbours: near, not far. The witnesses note the too late: your sin will find you out (Num. 32:23). The expositors observe the at the end of three days: the swift discovery. The expositors observe the they heard: the truth came out. The expositors observe the their neighbours: near, not far. The expositors observe the too late: your sin will find you out (Num. 32:23). The believer is taught to mark the at the end of three days, the they heard, the their neighbours, the too late, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that within three days the truth came out — the Gibeonites were near neighbours, discovered too late, the fraud unmasked when the oath was already sworn, the truth not hidden long (Num. 32:23).
17:
"And the children of Israel journeyed, and came unto their cities on the third day. Now their cities were Gibeon, and Chephirah, and Beeroth, and Kirjathjearim." The witnesses note Israel coming to the Gibeonite cities and confirming they were near at hand. The witnesses note that Gill notes not the whole camp, which remained at Gilgal, but a party who went to verify the report. The witnesses note that their cities were Gibeon, Chephirah, Beeroth, and Kirjath-jearim. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Israel journeyed — to verify; the came unto their cities — near at hand; the Gibeon and the rest — four towns; the the nearness laid bare — bound to those charged to be destroyed (Deut. 7:2).

The witnesses agree the report was put to proof and confirmed — Israel "came unto their cities," four towns close at hand, plainly within the forbidden land: the very nearness that the Gibeonites had laboured to hide was now seen with their own eyes; the naming of the four cities marks how substantial a people had slipped within the covenant by guile; it teaches that the consequences of an unadvised act are soon laid bare, and that what is done without God must be reckoned with in its results, the near cities a standing witness to the rash league, the people now bound to those they were charged to destroy (cf. Deut. 7:2).

So the witnesses make the verse their cities: The witnesses note the Israel journeyed: to verify. The witnesses note the came unto their cities: near at hand. The witnesses note the Gibeon and the rest: four towns. The witnesses note the the nearness laid bare: bound to those charged to be destroyed (Deut. 7:2). The expositors observe the Israel journeyed: to verify. The expositors observe the came unto their cities: near at hand. The expositors observe the Gibeon and the rest: four towns. The expositors observe the the nearness laid bare: bound to those charged to be destroyed (Deut. 7:2). The believer is taught to mark the Israel journeyed, the came unto their cities, the Gibeon and the rest, the the nearness laid bare, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that Israel came to the Gibeonite cities and found them near — the hidden nearness now seen, the consequence of the unadvised act laid bare, the people bound to those they were charged to destroy (Deut. 7:2).
18:
"And the children of Israel smote them not, because the princes of the congregation had sworn unto them by the LORD God of Israel. And all the congregation murmured against the princes." The witnesses note Israel sparing the Gibeonites because of the oath, while the congregation murmured against the princes. The witnesses note that Gill notes they smote them not, though they found the report true and that they were imposed upon. The witnesses note that Clarke notes all the congregation murmured. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the smote them not — for the oath's sake; the sworn by the LORD — the name pledged; the the congregation murmured — against the princes; the the oath sacred — not to take his name in vain (Exod. 20:7).

The witnesses agree the oath held even against the people's anger — Israel "smote them not, because the princes... had sworn... by the LORD," though "all the congregation murmured"; the binding force of a vow made in God's name overrode both the fraud that obtained it and the people's desire for the spoil; the princes would not break faith, even with deceivers, because the name of the LORD had been pledged; it teaches that an oath sworn by God's name is sacred and not to be broken, even when made unwisely or to the unworthy, the honour of that name being more than the people's gain, "thou shalt not take the name of the LORD thy God in vain" (Exod. 20:7) forbidding a broken oath as much as a false one.

So the witnesses make the verse the oath held: The witnesses note the smote them not: for the oath's sake. The witnesses note the sworn by the LORD: the name pledged. The witnesses note the the congregation murmured: against the princes. The witnesses note the the oath sacred: not to take his name in vain (Exod. 20:7). The expositors observe the smote them not: for the oath's sake. The expositors observe the sworn by the LORD: the name pledged. The expositors observe the the congregation murmured: against the princes. The expositors observe the the oath sacred: not to take his name in vain (Exod. 20:7). The believer is taught to mark the smote them not, the sworn by the LORD, the the congregation murmured, the the oath sacred, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that Israel spared the Gibeonites for the oath's sake, though the people murmured — the vow sworn by God's name binding even against fraud and the desire for spoil, his name not to be taken in vain by a broken oath (Exod. 20:7).
19:
"But all the princes said unto all the congregation, We have sworn unto them by the LORD God of Israel: now therefore we may not touch them." The witnesses note the princes standing firm — they have sworn by the LORD and may not touch the Gibeonites. The witnesses note that Gill notes the princes who went to Gibeon addressed all the Israelites there. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the princes pleading the oath sworn by the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the princes said — firm against the murmur; the we have sworn — by the LORD; the we may not touch them — the oath inviolable; the integrity over gain — who shall abide? he that changeth not (Ps. 15:1-4).

The witnesses agree the princes met the people's murmuring with the plain ground of the oath: "we have sworn unto them by the LORD... we may not touch them"; though deceived, they held their word inviolable because the LORD's name was in it, refusing to let even a just grievance lead them into perjury; the firmness of the princes in honouring the oath, against the popular clamour, was an act of true integrity; it teaches that the people of God must keep their word even when it costs them, holding faith sacred above advantage, "lord, who shall abide in thy tabernacle?... he that sweareth to his own hurt, and changeth not" (Ps. 15:1-4), the princes choosing integrity over gain.

So the witnesses make the verse we may not touch them: The witnesses note the all the princes said: firm against the murmur. The witnesses note the we have sworn: by the LORD. The witnesses note the we may not touch them: the oath inviolable. The witnesses note the integrity over gain: who shall abide? he that changeth not (Ps. 15:1-4). The expositors observe the all the princes said: firm against the murmur. The expositors observe the we have sworn: by the LORD. The expositors observe the we may not touch them: the oath inviolable. The expositors observe the integrity over gain: who shall abide? he that changeth not (Ps. 15:1-4). The believer is taught to mark the all the princes said, the we have sworn, the we may not touch them, the integrity over gain, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that the princes stood firm — having sworn by the LORD, they might not touch the Gibeonites — holding their word inviolable though deceived, choosing integrity over advantage, abiding by the oath sworn to their own hurt (Ps. 15:1-4).
20:
"This we will do to them; we will even let them live, lest wrath be upon us, because of the oath which we sware unto them." The witnesses note the resolve to let the Gibeonites live, lest wrath fall for breaking the oath. The witnesses note that Gill notes either this favour — preserving their lives — or the punishment of making them hewers of wood, was in their minds. The witnesses note that they would let them live, lest wrath be upon them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we will let them live — the resolve; the lest wrath be upon us — the fear of God; the because of the oath — sworn in his name; the perjury provoking God — famine for the broken oath (2 Sam. 21:1).

The witnesses agree the princes feared God's wrath more than the people's displeasure: "we will even let them live, lest wrath be upon us, because of the oath"; they reckoned that to break a vow sworn in the LORD's name would bring down his anger on the whole nation, a heavier evil than sparing the deceivers; their fear of perjury was a holy fear, esteeming the breach of an oath a sin God would surely punish; it teaches that the breaking of a solemn vow provokes God, and that his people should dread it as a real guilt, the after-history proving it true when famine came on Israel for Saul's slaying of these same Gibeonites (2 Sam. 21:1), the oath's breach bringing wrath.

So the witnesses make the verse lest wrath be upon us: The witnesses note the we will let them live: the resolve. The witnesses note the lest wrath be upon us: the fear of God. The witnesses note the because of the oath: sworn in his name. The witnesses note the perjury provoking God: famine for the broken oath (2 Sam. 21:1). The expositors observe the we will let them live: the resolve. The expositors observe the lest wrath be upon us: the fear of God. The expositors observe the because of the oath: sworn in his name. The expositors observe the perjury provoking God: famine for the broken oath (2 Sam. 21:1). The believer is taught to mark the we will let them live, the lest wrath be upon us, the because of the oath, the perjury provoking God, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that the princes would let the Gibeonites live, lest wrath fall for the broken oath — fearing God's anger more than the people's, the breach of a vow a real guilt, as later proved when famine came for its breaking (2 Sam. 21:1).
21:
"And the princes said unto them, Let them live; but let them be hewers of wood and drawers of water unto all the congregation; as the princes had promised them." The witnesses note the sentence — the Gibeonites to live, but be hewers of wood and drawers of water for the congregation. The witnesses note that Gill notes the princes were pressing and importunate to save their lives because of the oath. The witnesses note that Clarke notes "hewers of wood and drawers of water" may be a proverbial expression for the lowest labour. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the let them live — the oath honoured; the hewers of wood — the lowest labour; the drawers of water — the meanest service; the mercy with consequence — spared yet humbled (Nah. 1:12).

The witnesses agree the issue joined mercy with just consequence — life spared, but in lowly servitude: "let them live; but let them be hewers of wood and drawers of water"; the oath secured their lives, but their deceit fixed them in the meanest service, Clarke noting the phrase for the lowest labour; the sentence honoured the vow yet did not leave the fraud unpunished; it teaches that God's mercy may spare the deceiver yet appoint him a humbler place, the consequence of sin abiding even where life is granted, "though I have afflicted thee, I will afflict thee no more" (cf. Nah. 1:12), the Gibeonites kept alive but made servants.

So the witnesses make the verse hewers of wood: The witnesses note the let them live: the oath honoured. The witnesses note the hewers of wood: the lowest labour. The witnesses note the drawers of water: the meanest service. The witnesses note the mercy with consequence: spared yet humbled (Nah. 1:12). The expositors observe the let them live: the oath honoured. The expositors observe the hewers of wood: the lowest labour. The expositors observe the drawers of water: the meanest service. The expositors observe the mercy with consequence: spared yet humbled (Nah. 1:12). The believer is taught to mark the let them live, the hewers of wood, the drawers of water, the mercy with consequence, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites were to live but be hewers of wood and drawers of water — mercy joined with just consequence, life spared yet the deceit fixing them in the meanest service (Nah. 1:12).
22:
"And Joshua called for them, and he spake unto them, saying, Wherefore have ye beguiled us, saying, We are very far from you; when ye dwell among us?" The witnesses note Joshua summoning the Gibeonites and charging them with their deceit. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Gibeonites, who came as ambassadors, were detained at Gilgal till Israel returned, and Joshua then charged them. The witnesses note that he asked why they had beguiled Israel. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Joshua called for them — to account; the wherefore beguiled us — the deceit charged; the we are very far — their lie named; the reproof with mercy — rebuke and he will love thee (Prov. 9:8).

The witnesses agree Joshua brought the deceivers to account, charging them plainly: "wherefore have ye beguiled us... when ye dwell among us?"; though the oath bound him to spare them, he would not let the fraud pass unrebuked, calling sin by its name; the leader's open reproof shows that mercy and faithfulness need not excuse the wrong, the deceit named even as the life is spared; it teaches that the people of God may keep their word to a deceiver yet rebuke his deceit, dealing truly even with the false, "rebuke a wise man, and he will love thee" (Prov. 9:8), Joshua charging the Gibeonites with their guile.

So the witnesses make the verse wherefore beguiled us: The witnesses note the Joshua called for them: to account. The witnesses note the wherefore beguiled us: the deceit charged. The witnesses note the we are very far: their lie named. The witnesses note the reproof with mercy: rebuke and he will love thee (Prov. 9:8). The expositors observe the Joshua called for them: to account. The expositors observe the wherefore beguiled us: the deceit charged. The expositors observe the we are very far: their lie named. The expositors observe the reproof with mercy: rebuke and he will love thee (Prov. 9:8). The believer is taught to mark the Joshua called for them, the wherefore beguiled us, the we are very far, the reproof with mercy, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that Joshua summoned the Gibeonites and charged them with their deceit — the fraud rebuked even as the life is spared, mercy and faithfulness not excusing the wrong, sin called by its name (Prov. 9:8).
23:
"Now therefore ye are cursed, and there shall none of you be freed from being bondmen, and hewers of wood and drawers of water for the house of my God." The witnesses note the sentence — the Gibeonites cursed to perpetual bond-service for the house of God. The witnesses note that Gill notes they appeared to be the posterity of cursed Canaan, and, despite their artifice, should not escape the curse: "a servant of servants" — becoming servants of the Levites. The witnesses note that they were to be bondmen for the house of God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the ye are cursed — the curse on Canaan; the bondmen — perpetual servitude; the for the house of my God — near the sanctuary; the the curse turned to mercy — strangers joined to serve the LORD (Isa. 56:6).

The witnesses agree the sentence bound the Gibeonites to perpetual service, yet — by a remarkable turn — that service was "for the house of my God": Gill links it to the curse on Canaan, "a servant of servants" (Gen. 9:25), now fulfilled in their bondage; but the place of that bondage was the very sanctuary, near to God and his altar; what looked a curse became a strange mercy, the Canaanites drawn into the service of the LORD; it teaches that God's providence can turn a curse toward blessing, the lowly servants of his house brought nearer to him than the proud kings who perished — a foreshadowing of the Gentiles brought near to serve, "strangers... that join themselves to the LORD, to serve him" (Isa. 56:6).

So the witnesses make the verse bondmen for the house of God: The witnesses note the ye are cursed: the curse on Canaan. The witnesses note the bondmen: perpetual servitude. The witnesses note the for the house of my God: near the sanctuary. The witnesses note the the curse turned to mercy: strangers joined to serve the LORD (Isa. 56:6). The expositors observe the ye are cursed: the curse on Canaan. The expositors observe the bondmen: perpetual servitude. The expositors observe the for the house of my God: near the sanctuary. The expositors observe the the curse turned to mercy: strangers joined to serve the LORD (Isa. 56:6). The believer is taught to mark the ye are cursed, the bondmen, the for the house of my God, the the curse turned to mercy, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites were cursed to bond-service for the house of God — the curse on Canaan fulfilled, yet turned to a strange mercy, the Canaanites drawn near to serve the LORD, a foreshadowing of strangers joined to him (Isa. 56:6).
24:
"And they answered Joshua, and said, Because it was certainly told thy servants, how that the LORD thy God commanded his servant Moses to give you all the land, and to destroy all the inhabitants... therefore we were sore afraid of our lives because of you..." The witnesses note the Gibeonites explaining — they heard God had given Israel the land and feared for their lives. The witnesses note that Gill notes they had often heard of it by one means or another, not only certainly but frequently told. The witnesses note that Clarke notes self-preservation: they were sore afraid of their lives. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the it was certainly told — the report believed; the the LORD commanded Moses — to give the land; the to destroy all — the threatened doom; the sore afraid — flawed faith seeking refuge (Heb. 11:31).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites' defence reveals a real, if imperfect, faith — they believed the report of God's purpose and feared his power: "it was certainly told... how the LORD... commanded... to destroy all the inhabitants... therefore we were sore afraid"; their conviction that the LORD would surely give Israel the land, like Rahab's, moved them to seek life, though by deceit; the faith was true even where the means was crooked; it teaches that a genuine dread of God may work in the worst, and that even fearful, flawed faith reaches out for refuge, "by faith the harlot Rahab perished not" (Heb. 11:31), the Gibeonites believing the report and seeking to live.

So the witnesses make the verse sore afraid: The witnesses note the it was certainly told: the report believed. The witnesses note the the LORD commanded Moses: to give the land. The witnesses note the to destroy all: the threatened doom. The witnesses note the sore afraid: flawed faith seeking refuge (Heb. 11:31). The expositors observe the it was certainly told: the report believed. The expositors observe the the LORD commanded Moses: to give the land. The expositors observe the to destroy all: the threatened doom. The expositors observe the sore afraid: flawed faith seeking refuge (Heb. 11:31). The believer is taught to mark the it was certainly told, the the LORD commanded Moses, the to destroy all, the sore afraid, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites explained their fear — they believed God had given Israel the land and dreaded for their lives — a real if imperfect faith, like Rahab's, reaching for refuge though by crooked means (Heb. 11:31).
25:
"And now, behold, we are in thine hand: as it seemeth good and right unto thee to do unto us, do." The witnesses note the Gibeonites' submission — they cast themselves wholly on Joshua's mercy. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were in his power and at his disposal, ready to submit to whatever might be enjoined. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set their surrender — do as seemeth good and right. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we are in thine hand — wholly submitted; the as it seemeth good — to thy will; the do unto us — casting on mercy; the the posture of submission — let him do as seemeth good (2 Sam. 15:26).

The witnesses agree the Gibeonites yielded themselves entirely to Joshua's will: "we are in thine hand: as it seemeth good and right unto thee... do"; their unreserved submission, casting themselves on his mercy and justice, was the wisest course left them, and not unlike the surrender of faith to God; the sinner detected and helpless can do no better than place himself wholly in the hands of him who judges; it teaches the posture of true submission — to commit oneself to the mercy of the LORD, owning his right to dispose, "behold, here am I; let him do to me as seemeth good unto him" (2 Sam. 15:26), the Gibeonites resting on Joshua's good pleasure.

So the witnesses make the verse in thine hand: The witnesses note the we are in thine hand: wholly submitted. The witnesses note the as it seemeth good: to thy will. The witnesses note the do unto us: casting on mercy. The witnesses note the the posture of submission: let him do as seemeth good (2 Sam. 15:26). The expositors observe the we are in thine hand: wholly submitted. The expositors observe the as it seemeth good: to thy will. The expositors observe the do unto us: casting on mercy. The expositors observe the the posture of submission: let him do as seemeth good (2 Sam. 15:26). The believer is taught to mark the we are in thine hand, the as it seemeth good, the do unto us, the the posture of submission, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that the Gibeonites cast themselves wholly on Joshua's mercy — the unreserved submission of the helpless, not unlike the surrender of faith to God, let him do as seemeth good (2 Sam. 15:26).
26:
"And so did he unto them, and delivered them out of the hand of the children of Israel, that they slew them not." The witnesses note Joshua delivering the Gibeonites from the people, that they should not be slain. The witnesses note that Gill notes he did what was good and right, showing them favour and doing them justice. The witnesses note that Clarke notes he acted according to justice and mercy, delivering them so the people slew them not. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the so did he unto them — kept his word; the delivered them — from the people; the that they slew them not — protected from death; the mercy and truth — met together in the oath (Ps. 85:10).

The witnesses agree Joshua honoured the oath and protected the Gibeonites from the angry people: "he... delivered them out of the hand of the children of Israel, that they slew them not"; Clarke marks that he acted "according to justice and mercy," keeping faith with the deceivers and shielding them from death; the leader's steadfastness in his word, even against the popular will, vindicated the sanctity of the oath; it teaches that the people of God are to keep their pledged faith and protect even the unworthy whom they have promised to spare, mercy and truth meeting in the keeping of a vow, "mercy and truth are met together" (Ps. 85:10), Joshua delivering those he had sworn to spare.

So the witnesses make the verse delivered them: The witnesses note the so did he unto them: kept his word. The witnesses note the delivered them: from the people. The witnesses note the that they slew them not: protected from death. The witnesses note the mercy and truth: met together in the oath (Ps. 85:10). The expositors observe the so did he unto them: kept his word. The expositors observe the delivered them: from the people. The expositors observe the that they slew them not: protected from death. The expositors observe the mercy and truth: met together in the oath (Ps. 85:10). The believer is taught to mark the so did he unto them, the delivered them, the that they slew them not, the mercy and truth, and to keep an oath though made to his own hurt. The verse thus records that Joshua delivered the Gibeonites from the people, that they should not be slain — keeping his pledged faith and shielding even the unworthy, mercy and truth meeting in the keeping of the vow (Ps. 85:10).
27:
"And Joshua made them that day hewers of wood and drawers of water for the congregation, and for the altar of the LORD, even unto this day, in the place which he should choose." The witnesses note the Gibeonites appointed hewers of wood and drawers of water for the congregation and the altar of the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes Joshua constituted them in this office; hence some think they had the name Nethinim, persons given to the Levites for the service of the sanctuary. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set their service for the congregation and the altar, in the place the LORD should choose. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the hewers of wood — their lowly toil; the for the congregation — and the altar; the the Nethinim — given to the sanctuary; the nearness in lowliness — a doorkeeper in God's house (Ps. 84:10).

The witnesses agree the chapter ends with the Gibeonites settled in a service that was at once their punishment and their privilege — "hewers of wood and drawers of water for the congregation, and for the altar of the LORD": Gill connects them with the later "Nethinim," the given ones who served at the sanctuary; their lowly toil was now rendered to God's house and altar, drawing them near to his worship; the curse on Canaan was thus overruled to a place of nearness to the LORD; it teaches that God's providence can turn even a deceiver's humiliation into a station of service near his altar, the lowest place in his house better than the heights of the perishing world, "a day in thy courts is better than a thousand... I had rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God" (Ps. 84:10).

So the witnesses make the verse for the altar of the LORD: The witnesses note the hewers of wood: their lowly toil. The witnesses note the for the congregation: and the altar. The witnesses note the the Nethinim: given to the sanctuary. The witnesses note the nearness in lowliness: a doorkeeper in God's house (Ps. 84:10). The expositors observe the hewers of wood: their lowly toil. The expositors observe the for the congregation: and the altar. The expositors observe the the Nethinim: given to the sanctuary. The expositors observe the nearness in lowliness: a doorkeeper in God's house (Ps. 84:10). The verse thus records that the Gibeonites were made servants for the congregation and the altar of the LORD — their humiliation overruled to a place of nearness to God's house, the lowest service near his altar better than the heights of the world, a doorkeeper in the house of God (Ps. 84:10).
